input.content:
# Pitch

**The Breakwater Season** is a literary novel set in the fictional coastal town of Coldwater Reach, Maine, from autumn 1943 through spring 1945. Three people — Eira Bowman, a fifty-two-year-old woman who holds the harbormaster post no man wanted after Pearl Harbor; Hannah Pfeiffer, a seventeen-year-old daughter of Bavarian immigrants whose dairy farm now houses four German POW laborers; and Dr. Marcus Klein, a Viennese Jewish scholar teaching high-school German in the town that hired him because it had no other choice — each carry, separately and without coordination, a single unresolved event: an unidentified man's body that washed onto the Coldwater breakwater in October 1943, the day after Eira saw something on the dock she has told no one about. Over eighteen months, each character's investigation of that event is subordinate to an internal reckoning that was already underway before the body arrived: Eira with the cost of holding authority in a body the authority was never designed for; Hannah with the slow accumulation of small choices that will determine who she becomes; Marcus with whether displacement is permanent, and what remains of a person when it ends. The war does not intrude as adventure. It arrives as a radio crackle, a casualty telegram, a ration book running low, four young men working silently in a cold field. The story ends in spring 1945, just before VE Day, with resolutions that are smaller and older than the war's ending — and at least one question the reader assembles but no character fully answers.

# World

## Coldwater Reach, Maine — 1943–1945

### Geography and Physical Character

Coldwater Reach is a working harbor town on the mid-Maine coast — not a resort town, not a lobster-postcard town. Population roughly 1,400. The harbor is deepwater enough for fishing draggers and the occasional coastal freighter carrying lumber and canned goods south. The breakwater is granite, built in the 1890s, about four hundred feet long, stained orange-brown at the tide line. The harbormaster's office is a two-room clapboard structure at the landward end of the wharf, wood-stove heated, smelling of diesel and wet rope and the tide-ledger that Eira Bowman keeps in a hand ruled notebook. A single telephone line. A BC-1206 shortwave receiver on a shelf above the window.

The town sits at the mouth of the Reach — a tidal inlet that narrows to a river a mile upstream. Main Street runs parallel to the water: a general store, a post office, a pharmacy that doubles as the soda fountain and rumor exchange, a hardware store, a Congregationalist church, a Catholic church (mostly for the Franco-American families on the north side), and the consolidated district school where Marcus Klein teaches. The school is a 1927 brick building with four classrooms and a furnace that requires tending.

Three miles inland, the Pfeiffer farm occupies 140 acres of rolling pasture and second-growth woodlot above the frost line. The icehouse on the eastern edge of the property — a low timber structure with sawdust insulation, emptied of its summer-cut stock — has been converted under county direction into a barracks for four German POWs. Two cots on each side, a cast-iron stove, a single electric bulb on a long cord.

### Period Texture

**The war's presence on the home front:** No neon. Dimout regulations on the coast through early 1943 (the U-boat threat has receded but habits persist). Windows taped at night. Gasoline rationed — Eira drives a 1937 Ford half-ton on a C-ration card she stretches. Meat, butter, and sugar rationed. Ration books are carried like wallets. Silk and nylon unavailable. The soda fountain has no Coca-Cola some weeks.

V-Mail: letters home from servicemen are microfilmed, shipped, reprinted at the other end — small, sometimes slightly blurred. Families keep them in shoeboxes. The pharmacist's son is in the Pacific; the envelopes on the counter are from the early months, before everything went quiet.

The draft has taken every able-bodied man between twenty and thirty-five. The town runs on men too old, men too young, men physically deferred, and women doing work that has no name yet in the town's vocabulary.

**Radio:** The battery-powered Philco in the Pfeiffer kitchen plays WBZ Boston on clear nights. Marcus has a smaller Zenith portable in his room at the Crow house. Eira's office shortwave brings in Portland and, on good nights, New York. The war arrives as voice, not as newsprint — though the weekly *Downeast Courier* carries the names of local men in the casualty columns. Everyone reads those first.

**The POW labor program:** Authorized under the Geneva Convention and expanded aggressively by 1943 as American farm labor dried up. The central camp for Maine is at Houlton, in Aroostook County; satellite work details are dispatched to farms and canneries. Four prisoners assigned to Pfeiffer farm are escorted daily by Lonzo Reed, a sixty-year-old Coastal Patrol private with a 1903 Springfield he has not fired in two decades. The prisoners are paid eighty cents a day in scrip redeemable at the camp canteen. They are not chained. They are watched.

### Sensory Register

- October at the harbor: diesel smoke, salt brine, the smell of fish racks drying, fog that arrives before dawn and burns off by nine. The breakwater slick with kelp at low tide.
- The Pfeiffer farm in November: manure and hay, the low-frequency sound of cows before morning milking, mud frozen at the surface and soft underneath, wood-smoke from the farmhouse chimney mixing with cold air.
- Marcus's classroom in winter: chalk dust, the radiator's tick and hiss, the particular silence of teenagers watching a man who sounds different.
- Henry Crow's house, where Marcus boards: the smell of Lillian's cooking (dried beans, cornbread, stewed tomatoes from her summer put-up), the warmth of a household that does not perform welcome because the welcome is actual.

### Mood

Autumnal, self-contained, coastal-provincial. The war exists as a pressure rather than an event — felt in absences, in the cost of things, in who is and isn't at the table. The prose treats the landscape as a working environment, not a backdrop. Weather matters because it changes what is possible. The Reach matters because it is the town's reason for existing. The body on the breakwater does not shatter this world; it disturbs it, the way a stone disturbs water — the rings spreading slowly, over months.

# Characters

---

## POV Characters

### Eira Bowman
**Age:** 52 at the story's opening (October 1943)  
**Role:** Harbormaster of Coldwater Reach; primary POV character  
**Background:** Born in Coldwater Reach. Married Owen Bowman at twenty-three. Worked beside him at the harbor for twenty years before his death in the November gale of 1935. The selectmen gave her the position provisionally — for one season, until they found someone. Eight years later she is still provisional in no way she acknowledges. No children. A sister in Bangor she corresponds with by letter.

**Inner drives:** Eira's deepest want is to be neither exceptional nor invisible — to be simply competent, which she is, and to have that competence be the end of the conversation. The job continuously refuses her that. Every decision she makes carries the freight of its being made by her. She manages this by making the job entirely procedural in her own mind: tide tables, draft limits, mooring fees, storm protocol. Her grief for Owen has been so thoroughly converted into harbor management that she sometimes cannot locate it directly; it surfaces only in technical language, which is to say, it surfaces all the time.

**Voice:** Spare. Observational. Economical with interior commentary. She notices the physical world with the precision of someone who has had to act on what she notices. When she is frightened she makes lists. When she is angry she goes quiet. When she is uncertain she delays a decision by measuring something.

**The inciting secret:** On the night of October 11, 1943, Eira saw a man she did not recognize on the north side of the wharf at 2 a.m. — checking mooring lines, or appearing to. She did not hail him because she was not certain enough of what she saw. She has not told the Coast Guard this. The body came in three days later.

**Relationships:**  
- *Owen Bowman (deceased):* The ghost in all her professional habits. She does not idealize him.  
- *The fishermen and dockhands:* Mutual competence-based respect; not warmth.  
- *The selectmen:* A managed tension. They need her to be right. She needs them to say nothing.  
- *Marcus Klein:* They know each other by sight and one professional encounter (a licensing matter). They become something more careful than friends.  
- *Hannah Pfeiffer:* They are strangers until the body creates a proximity neither sought.

---

### Hannah Pfeiffer
**Age:** 17 at the story's opening  
**Role:** Farmer's daughter; second POV character  
**Background:** Born in Coldwater Reach to Albrecht and Magda Pfeiffer, who emigrated from Bavaria in 1922. She has an older brother, Wilhelm (called Will), who enlisted in 1942 and is currently somewhere in the Pacific. Hannah has grown up bilingual in a household that is now trying to be only one thing at a time. She attends the consolidated school where Marcus teaches; he is her German and Latin instructor.

**Inner drives:** Hannah wants, with a seventeen-year-old's specific intensity, to make a choice that is entirely her own — not her father's, not the war's, not the town's assumption about who the German dairy farmer's daughter must be. She is intelligent and observant in ways the farm does not fully use. The arrival of the POWs disrupts a status quo she was already restless inside.

**The Karl question:** Karl Reinmar, 19, the youngest of the four POWs, is from Kirchheim, a town fifty kilometers from Albrecht Pfeiffer's hometown of Amberg. He speaks Bavarian-inflected German that Albrecht recognizes by sound. Hannah speaks to Karl through the fence in German, first about farm work, then about other things. This is neither romantic fantasy nor spy thriller; it is the strange intimacy of two young people on opposite sides of a fence who share a language and a predicament neither of them chose.

**Voice:** The most interior of the three. Her narration lets things in — intrusive thoughts, half-formed impressions, the German phrases her father uses at the table that she finds herself thinking in. She is not unreliable, but she is unguarded in ways Eira and Marcus are not. The farm and its rhythms give her chapters a ground note of physical work: milking, fencing, feeding, mucking, the cold.

**Relationships:**  
- *Albrecht Pfeiffer (father):* She loves him and she is losing him — to the war's effect on his sense of self, to his retreat into German, to his silence about what he did or did not do before 1922. He is not a villain. He is a man who chose the wrong time to stop speaking.  
- *Magda Pfeiffer (mother):* Magda's silence is different from Albrecht's — it is a withdrawal, not a protest. Hannah tends her practically, without being able to reach her.  
- *Karl Reinmar:* The connection she allows herself that she is not allowed to allow. It does not resolve simply.  
- *Will Pfeiffer (brother):* Absent. His V-Mails are getting shorter. His last letter was in August. Hannah carries this the way you carry something heavy when you've decided not to put it down.  
- *Marcus Klein:* Her teacher. She is one of perhaps three students who read what he assigns. He notices her; she notices that he notices.

---

### Dr. Marcus Klein
**Age:** 47 at the story's opening  
**Role:** German and Latin teacher at Coldwater Reach Consolidated School; third POV character  
**Background:** Born in Vienna, 1896. Classical philology at the University of Vienna. Left Austria in March 1938, four days after the Anschluss, for Paris. From Paris to London in 1940 when France fell. Interned in Canada at Camp Sherbrooke (an enemy alien internment camp in Quebec for German-speaking refugees) from June to December 1940, released after advocacy by the Emergency Committee in Aid of Displaced Foreign Scholars. Arrived in the United States in January 1941. Took up a nominal post at Harvard Extension while living on the Committee's rolls. Hired by the Coldwater school board in March 1942 after the previous German instructor (a twenty-four-year-old local named Braddock) enlisted. He is Jewish; the school board has not asked; he has not said.

**Inner drives:** Marcus wants to stop waiting. His entire life since 1938 has been provisional — the next city, the next permit, the next sponsoring organization, the next temporary berth. Coldwater is the first place he has lived two years in a row since Vienna. He cannot tell yet if this is an ending or just a longer pause. His deepest question is whether he has an identity that is not constructed entirely from what he was displaced from — and whether a person can build anything new at forty-seven.

**The Sherbrooke question:** The internment camp is a closed subject in his narration, mentioned in a single early chapter and thereafter avoided. This avoidance is itself information. He was interned with other refugees, not with Nazis, but the distinction did not concern the Canadian government, and the experience of being interned under suspicion of loyalty to the country that had just expelled him is a wound he manages by precision — by keeping his language so controlled that nothing can be used against him again.

**Voice:** Precise, slightly formal, with a measured academic quality. He thinks in terms of categories and distinctions. His interior monologue often takes the form of argument-structure — a proposition, a counterproposition, a qualification. His relationship to English is the relationship of a man who mastered it as a foreign language and now trusts it more than the languages he was born into: he knows exactly what each word costs.

**Relationships:**  
- *Henry Crow:* The one person in town with whom Marcus does not feel observed. Henry is not performing tolerance; he simply does not need Marcus to be explicable. They play chess on Thursday evenings.  
- *Lillian Crow:* She feeds Marcus, and feeds him well, and asks no questions. He is faintly undone by ordinary domestic kindness.  
- *Hannah Pfeiffer:* His best student. He is protective of her in the way of a teacher who sees a mind being wasted. The POW situation on her farm adds a complication.  
- *Eira Bowman:* They are, separately, the two most anomalous figures in Coldwater — each holding a post that the town didn't build for them. Their recognition of each other is slow and never named.  
- *The school board:* They are waiting for him to fail, or for the war to end, whichever comes first. He knows this and teaches around it.

---

## Secondary Characters

### Henry Crow
**Age:** 54  
**Role:** Chief custodian of Coldwater Reach Consolidated School; Marcus's landlord  
**Background:** Born in Norfolk, Virginia. Came north with his wife Lillian in 1924 as part of the Second Great Migration. Has been at the school since 1927. He repairs everything in the building and is the only person who knows where the water shutoffs are.  
**Voice (if a POV chapter is used):** Dry, practical, grounded. He has outlasted several principals. He understands the town's architecture of power and moves through it without appearing to notice it — which is not the same as not noticing. His perspective on Marcus is the most accurate in the book and the least sentimental.  
**Relationship to Eira:** They know each other by long acquaintance. Henry's son Elias is in the Navy.

### Lillian Crow
**Age:** 51  
**Role:** Henry's wife; keeper of the Crow household  
**Background:** Also originally from Norfolk. Works part-time at the canning plant on the south end of town. Manages the household, the garden, the put-up, and the quiet.  
**Function:** Lillian is the novel's primary figure of uncalculated human warmth. She does not have chapters but her presence is load-bearing in Marcus's arc.

### Lonzo Reed
**Age:** 60  
**Role:** Coastal Patrol private assigned as escort/guard for the Pfeiffer farm POW detail  
**Background:** A Coldwater native who served in France in 1918, came back, worked at the hardware store for twenty years, and was called back into service at fifty-eight because they needed warm bodies on the coast watch. He is not cruel to the prisoners. He is not kind to them either. He is tired and his feet hurt and he would like very much to go home by four o'clock.  
**Voice (if a POV chapter is used):** Laconic. He has seen what war does to people and has arranged his mind around not thinking about it. His observations of the Pfeiffer family, the prisoners, and Hannah in particular carry a long-view weight that none of the main POVs can achieve.

### Karl Reinmar
**Age:** 19  
**Role:** One of four German POWs assigned to Pfeiffer farm; the youngest; the one Hannah speaks to  
**Background:** From Kirchheim, Bavaria. Conscripted at seventeen. Captured in North Africa with Rommel's Afrika Korps in May 1943. He speaks German with the Bavarian accent that Albrecht Pfeiffer recognizes. He is not a Nazi ideologue; he is also not innocent of the war's logic. He is nineteen.  
**Voice (if a POV chapter is used):** Simple declarative German translated into English — his English is minimal. His chapter, if it exists, would be among the shortest and would focus on a single day.

### Albrecht Pfeiffer
**Age:** 58  
**Role:** Hannah's father; owner of Pfeiffer farm  
**Background:** Born in Amberg, Bavaria. Emigrated to Maine in 1922 with Magda. Has farmed the Reach for twenty years. His silence since the war started is not treason; it is a man's grief at being made into a category he does not recognize. He has not returned to Germany since leaving. He does not know what he would find.

### Magda Pfeiffer
**Age:** 55  
**Role:** Hannah's mother  
**Background:** From a farm village twenty kilometers from Amberg. Her withdrawal is clinical by any name; the town has no name for it in 1943. She tends the house. She does not speak unless spoken to, and sometimes not then.

### Owen Bowman (deceased)
**Age at death:** 49 (drowned November 1935)  
**Role:** Eira's husband; the previous harbormaster  
**Function:** He is present in every chapter Eira has — in the harbor's routines, in her professional habits, in the way she notes what he would have done. He is not idealized and not blamed. He is simply absent in a way that has become structural.

# Style Guide

## Governing Principles

**POV:** Third-person limited past tense throughout. The narrative never leaves the head of the chapter's assigned POV character. No information the POV character could not have perceived or inferred. No head-hopping within a chapter, ever.

**Tense:** Past simple. No present-tense intrusions except in period documents rendered verbatim (V-Mail text, ledger entries, official notices).

**Narrative distance:** Close but not confessional. The camera sits slightly behind the POV character's eyes — we see what they see, feel what they feel, but the prose does not editorialize on their behalf. Trust the reader to feel what is not said. This is the single most important stylistic directive.

**Reflection vs. action:** Default to action, observation, and dialogue. Extended interior monologue should be rare and earned. When a character thinks, the thought should arise from something they are doing or have just done — not from a pause in the action designed to host reflection.

**Sentence rhythm:** Varied but weighted toward the short and declarative, especially in Eira's chapters. No sentence should be longer than the thought it is carrying. Avoid nested subordinate clauses for effect. Avoid free indirect discourse that turns too literary too fast.

**Dialogue:** Clipped across all three voices — period characters did not explain themselves at length. No dialogue tag other than *said* or *asked* unless the tone is genuinely ambiguous. No stage directions embedded in dialogue (*she said, turning to face him with a look of quiet desperation*). Let the line carry the weight.

**Period texture:** Use accurate period detail. Resist the urge to explain it. If a character picks up a ration book, write what they do with it, not what a ration book is. The reader is trusted to look up what they don't know. Never use an anachronistic word, phrase, or social assumption.

---

## Voice by Character

### Eira's Chapters

**Register:** Sparse. Technical. Procedural. Eira thinks in the language of harbor management: draft, displacement, tide tables, freeboard, weather windows, mooring configuration, licensing categories.

**Interior:** Minimal. When Eira reflects, it is brief and immediately converted into action or practical assessment. Her grief and her love and her anger live inside her professional judgments. If she is afraid, she measures something. If she is grieving, she runs through a checklist.

**Sentences:** Short to medium. Subject-verb-object. Few adverbs. She does not say *she felt a cold fear settle in her chest*; she says *she put down the log and went to the window.*

**The sea:** It is a workplace. It is not a metaphor. The harbor appears in specific, functional terms: the state of the tide, the behavior of the mooring lines, the color of the sky that means weather, the height of the chop. Any sentence that starts treating the sea as symbolic should be cut.

**Dialogue:** Clipped to the edge of abrupt. Eira says the minimum necessary. She is not cold; she is economical. Pauses and silences in her dialogue should be written as actions, not stage directions.

**Example register:**  
> The southeast corner mooring had dragged two feet. She noted it in the log and went to tell Dakin before he ran it on the tide. The fog was already in.

---

### Hannah's Chapters

**Register:** The most interior and porous of the three. Hannah's consciousness is allowed to be louder — she is seventeen, she is restless, she is paying attention to everything without knowing which things matter.

**German:** German words and phrases appear in Hannah's chapters wherever they would naturally surface in a bilingual childhood — her father's idioms, the names of things as she learned them at home, phrases she finds herself thinking in before she can find the English. These are italicized and never translated via the prose. Context must carry meaning, or let it stay untranslated. No phonetic spellings; no burlesque of accent.

**Interior:** More permitted than in Eira's or Marcus's chapters. Hannah can brood, digress, circle back. The farm is the world, and her narration should convey the way a specific physical world can feel infinite when you have never left it. But: her interior should always be anchored in sensory fact. She does not float; she digresses from something she is touching.

**Sentences:** More varied than Eira's — longer when she is absorbed, shorter when she is alarmed. Her prose can carry a run-on quality when her thoughts are racing, but it should not become stream of consciousness. The observer's intelligence underneath should always be findable.

**Dialogue:** Hannah's dialogue is more exploratory than Eira's. She asks more questions. When she speaks to Karl, the exchange is in German; render it in English but make clear it is German being spoken (an initial tag is enough; thereafter the prose assumes it).

**Example register:**  
> She brought the pail out at six and the prisoners were already up. Karl was at the far fence, looking at nothing she could see — just the tree line, the way it went pale before the sun did. *Der Morgen zieht sich*, her father used to say. Morning takes its time. She set the pail down and he didn't look over.

---

### Marcus's Chapters

**Register:** Precise, measured, slightly formal. Marcus's narration is the English of a man who learned it as an adult, trusted it for its precision, and has used that trust as a form of armor. His prose is exact in the way that academic prose is exact — he distinguishes; he qualifies; he refuses vagueness.

**Interior:** More present than Eira's, more controlled than Hannah's. Marcus thinks in argument-structures — he proposes, then questions, then qualifies. When he is uncomfortable, his narration goes slightly more formal, as though closing a door.

**Languages:** Marcus knows German, Latin, French, and English. In his narration, he may occasionally produce the German or Latin term before the English one — not because he doesn't know the English, but because the other word is more precise. These are italicized. He does not use German to be charming; he uses it because sometimes it is the right word.

**The Sherbrooke gap:** He does not narrate Sherbrooke directly. When something causes him to approach that memory — a barbed wire fence, a locked room, a bureaucrat's tone of studied neutrality — the prose may register the approach without entering. A white space, a cut, a sudden transition to practical action. The reader understands before Marcus names it.

**Sentences:** Longer than Eira's but never loose. His sentences should feel constructed — like a man who has thought about what he is going to say before saying it. Subordinate clauses are permitted when the idea requires it. No sentence should be longer than Marcus's patience with imprecision.

**Dialogue:** Formal and careful. He does not use contractions in formal speech; he uses them sparingly in private speech. His conversation is polite with professional distance, warmer with Henry and Lillian, careful with Hannah in the way of a teacher who does not want to overweight what he sees.

**Example register:**  
> He had been in Coldwater for nineteen months. Long enough, he thought, to recognize that recognition was a different matter. They knew him now — his face, his schedule, the car he didn't own. Whether they knew what he was remained open. He had stopped testing it.

---

## What to Avoid Across All Three Voices

- Metaphor that draws attention to itself
- Pathetic fallacy (the weather does not mirror the emotion)
- Any sentence that translates an image directly into its emotional meaning (*The empty harbor felt like her own grief.*)
- Characters explaining themselves to themselves
- Anachronistic diction (no *okay*, no *trauma*, no *processing*, no *boundaries*, no *really* as an intensifier)
- Dialect spellings or phonetic accent
- Omniscient intrusions — the narrator never knows more than the POV character

# Continuity

Hard facts that must not break in later drafting. These are binding.

1. The story runs from **October 1943 to spring 1945**, ending just before VE Day (May 8, 1945).
2. **Eira Bowman** is 52 in October 1943. She has been harbormaster of Coldwater Reach since 1936, following her husband Owen's death in the November gale of **November 1935**. Owen Bowman was the previous harbormaster.
3. **Hannah Pfeiffer** is 17 in October 1943. Her family farm is three miles inland from Coldwater Reach. Her brother **Will Pfeiffer** is serving in the Pacific. His last V-Mail arrived in August 1943.
4. **Dr. Marcus Klein** is 47 in October 1943. He is Viennese, Jewish, and was interned at **Camp Sherbrooke**, Quebec, from **June to December 1940**. He arrived in Coldwater in **March 1942**. He boards with Henry and Lillian Crow.
5. **Henry Crow** and **Lillian Crow** are Black. They came north from Norfolk, Virginia, in **1924**. Henry is the chief custodian of Coldwater Reach Consolidated School. He has a son, **Elias Crow**, currently serving in the Navy.
6. The body washes onto the **Coldwater breakwater** after a three-day blow, arriving on or around **October 14, 1943**. The man carries no identification. The Coast Guard takes the body. The body's identity is never definitively confirmed within the story — the reader assembles an answer across thirty chapters, but the underlying fact stays partly in shadow.
7. On the night of **October 11, 1943**, Eira saw an unidentified man on the north side of the wharf at approximately 2 a.m. She did not hail him. She did not report this to the Coast Guard.
8. The **German POW labor detail** on Pfeiffer farm consists of four prisoners, all captured in North Africa in 1943. The youngest is **Karl Reinmar**, 19, from Kirchheim, Bavaria — approximately 50 kilometers from Albrecht Pfeiffer's hometown of **Amberg, Bavaria**.
9. **Lonzo Reed**, 60, is the Coastal Patrol private assigned to escort the Pfeiffer farm POW detail. He is a Coldwater native and a veteran of the First World War.
10. The three main POV characters — Eira, Hannah, and Marcus — **never meet as a group of three**. Their threads cross in pairs, by accident or design, but there is no scene in which all three are present together.
11. There is **no spy plot, no Nazi agent, no coded communication, no submarine landing**. The body on the breakwater is a mystery of human circumstance, not espionage.
12. **Albrecht Pfeiffer** emigrated from Amberg, Bavaria, to Maine in **1922**. He has not returned to Germany.
13. The story has **30 chapters**, rotating POV across the three main characters (approximately ten chapters each). There may be 2–3 exceptional short chapters from a fourth perspective (Henry Crow, Lonzo Reed, or Karl Reinmar), but these are structural exceptions, not regular POV slots.
14. **At least one narrative thread must remain deliberately unresolved**. Each of the three characters resolves their *relation* to the breakwater question, but the underlying fact of who the dead man was may or may not be answered in full.
15. The POW labor program detail is historically accurate: German POWs were housed and used as farm labor throughout Maine in 1943–1945, under the auspices of the Geneva Convention, paid in camp scrip, and guarded by aged or deferred service personnel.
16. Marcus Klein's German and Latin teaching position at the school was created by the enlistment of the previous instructor, a local man named **Braddock**. The school board's employment of Marcus was pragmatic and remains grudging.
17. **Coldwater Reach** is a fictional town. Real Maine place names (Portland, Bangor, Houlton, Sherbrooke, Cambridge) may appear. Real people's names must not appear.

# Glossary

Alphabetical. Definitions are brief and specific to this story's usage.

---

**Albrecht Pfeiffer** — Hannah's father. Bavarian emigrant (Amberg, 1922). Dairy farmer. Has retreated from English into German since the war started. Age 58.

**Amberg** — Town in Bavaria, Germany, approximately 50 km from Kirchheim. Albrecht Pfeiffer's hometown.

**BC-1206** — A U.S. Navy-surplus shortwave radio receiver. Eira has one in the harbormaster's office, used to monitor harbor traffic and weather channels.

**Breakwater** — The granite jetty at the entrance to Coldwater Reach harbor, built ca. 1890s. Approximately 400 feet long. Site of the body's discovery in October 1943.

**Braddock** — The previous German and Latin instructor at Coldwater Reach Consolidated School. Enlisted in 1942; Marcus was hired to replace him.

**Camp Houlton** — Real: the main German POW internment and work camp in Aroostook County, Maine. Parent facility for satellite farm work details including the Pfeiffer farm detail.

**Camp Sherbrooke** — Real: an enemy alien internment camp in Sherbrooke, Quebec, operated by the Canadian government 1940–1943. Marcus Klein was interned here June–December 1940.

**Coldwater Reach** — Fictional. A deepwater harbor town on the mid-Maine coast. Population approximately 1,400. The story's primary setting.

**Coastal Patrol** — The U.S. Coastal Patrol (also called the Coastal Picket Patrol), a civilian auxiliary that patrolled coastal areas. Lonzo Reed serves as a private in this capacity.

**Elias Crow** — Henry and Lillian Crow's son. Currently serving in the U.S. Navy.

**Eira Bowman** — Harbormaster of Coldwater Reach. Age 52. POV character. The widow of Owen Bowman.

**Emergency Committee in Aid of Displaced Foreign Scholars** — Real: a U.S. organization (formally the Emergency Committee in Aid of Displaced German Scholars, later broadened) that helped refugee academics secure positions. Marcus was on their rolls 1940–1941.

**Hannah Pfeiffer** — Daughter of Albrecht and Magda Pfeiffer. Age 17. POV character.

**Henry Crow** — Chief custodian of Coldwater Reach Consolidated School. Marcus's landlord. Age 54. Black; came north from Norfolk in 1924.

**Karl Reinmar** — German POW. Age 19. From Kirchheim, Bavaria. The youngest of the four prisoners on the Pfeiffer farm detail. Captured in North Africa, May 1943.

**Kirchheim** — Town in Bavaria, Germany, approximately 50 km from Amberg. Karl Reinmar's hometown.

**Lillian Crow** — Henry Crow's wife. Age 51. Works at the canning plant. Central domestic presence in Marcus's arc.

**Lonzo Reed** — Coastal Patrol private. Age 60. Coldwater native, WWI veteran. Escort/guard for the Pfeiffer farm POW detail.

**Magda Pfeiffer** — Hannah's mother. Age 55. Has largely stopped speaking; her withdrawal is unnamed but clinical in retrospect.

**Marcus Klein, Dr.** — German and Latin teacher at Coldwater Reach Consolidated School. Age 47. Viennese. Jewish. POV character.

**Owen Bowman** — Eira's husband. Previous harbormaster of Coldwater Reach. Drowned in the November gale of 1935. Age at death: 49.

**Pfeiffer farm** — 140-acre dairy farm, three miles inland from Coldwater Reach. Property of Albrecht and Magda Pfeiffer. The POW icehouse is on the eastern edge.

**The Reach** — The tidal inlet on which Coldwater Reach sits; also used as shorthand for the town itself.

**V-Mail** — Victory Mail. A WWII U.S. postal system in which letters to and from servicemen were microfilmed, shipped, and reprinted at the receiving end. The originals were destroyed. The reprinted letters were small and sometimes slightly blurred.

**Will Pfeiffer** — Hannah's older brother. Enlisted 1942; currently serving in the Pacific. Last V-Mail received by the family: August 1943.

# Themes

## 1. Authority and the Body That Holds It

Eira Bowman holds a post that was built, over decades, for a man — not any man in particular, but a general category that excluded her before she was born. She did not take the job by fighting for it; she took it because the position was left and she was the one standing beside it. The story examines what it costs to hold authority in a body the institution did not design for, and whether competence can ever be fully separated from the performance of competence. Eira's arc is not about proving herself — she is past that — but about discovering what she is left with once she has spent twenty years converting her grief, her ambition, and her identity into a professional function. The body on the breakwater forces her to carry something that is not in the ledger, that has no column, that the job's procedures cannot process. This is the story's central test of her.

---

## 2. Displacement and the Question of When It Ends

Marcus Klein has been in motion since 1938. Five years, five cities, one internment. Every provisional berth has taught him not to unpack. Coldwater Reach is the first place he has been long enough to know the sound the school furnace makes in January, to know that Henry Crow takes his coffee without sugar, to know that the pharmacist's name is Doreen and she reads the casualty columns before opening the store. The theme is not whether Marcus belongs in Coldwater — the town will not settle that question for him. The theme is whether belonging can be rebuilt after it has been taken apart at the root, and what tools are available to a man who is forty-seven, whose first language is associated in the town's mind with the enemy, and who has learned through Sherbrooke that institutions do not protect persons when it is inconvenient for them to do so. His arc turns on the question: can a man stop waiting for the other shoe to drop without pretending the first one didn't?

---

## 3. The Accumulation of Small Choices

Hannah's story is not about one dramatic decision. It is about the way a person's character is formed by the dozens of small choices that accumulate across months — to speak or not, to cross a line or hold it, to protect a secret or name one. Adolescence in the novel is not sentimentalized as a time of innocence or mocked as a time of naïve emotion. It is rendered as the actual moral education it is: the period during which a person discovers what they will do when they think no one is watching. The war, the prisoners, her father's silence, her mother's withdrawal, Karl Reinmar at the fence line — all of these are contexts in which Hannah makes choices no one assigns her. The theme asks what kind of person those choices are making, and whether she is the one making them or they are making her.

---

## 4. What Is Not Said

Across all three POVs, the story's structural principle is restraint — the conviction that what is not named is often more load-bearing than what is. Eira does not say she misses Owen; she notes that the mooring on berth seven is two feet off and Owen would have caught it in October. Marcus does not say he is afraid; he declines to use the word *home* in any sentence that includes Coldwater Reach. Hannah does not say she is falling into something with Karl; she notes that she has memorized the sound of the latch on the icehouse door. The theme is not merely a stylistic preference; it is an argument about how people actually survive intense experience — by keeping it in a language close enough to the surface to be functional, and far enough from the center not to be disabling. The breakwater body is the story's master image for this: something that surfaced, was looked at briefly, and then was taken away. What remains is what each person does with what they know and have not said.

text: All seven files are written. Now I'll lock the most load-bearing facts into continuity.

facts: The story spans October 1943 to spring 1945 (just before VE Day, May 8, 1945). It must not begin earlier or end later.
The three POV characters — Eira Bowman (52), Hannah Pfeiffer (17), and Dr. Marcus Klein (47) — never appear together as a group of three in any scene.
The body washes onto the Coldwater breakwater around October 14, 1943. The body's identity is never definitively confirmed in the text; the reader assembles a partial answer across thirty chapters.
On the night of October 11, 1943, Eira saw an unidentified man on the north side of the wharf at ~2 a.m. and did not report it to the Coast Guard. This is her secret throughout the novel.

There is no spy plot. No Nazi agents, no coded messages, no submarine landings. The war enters through radio, letters, ration books, casualty columns, and the prisoners on the farm — not through plot mechanics.
Marcus Klein is Jewish. He was interned at Camp Sherbrooke, Quebec, June–December 1940. The internment is never narrated directly in his chapters — it is approached obliquely and cut away from.

Karl Reinmar (German POW, 19, from Kirchheim, Bavaria) and Hannah Pfeiffer interact in German across the fence line of the Pfeiffer farm icehouse. This relationship is not a romance plot; it is an intimacy of shared language and shared predicament.

Henry Crow and Lillian Crow are Black, came north from Norfolk in 1924, and are Marcus's landlords. Race is rendered as specific lived fact, not as a subject lecture. Henry's son Elias is in the Navy.
At least one thread must end deliberately unresolved. Each character resolves their relation to the breakwater question; the underlying fact of who the dead man was may stay in shadow.
The novel has exactly 30 chapters, ~80,000 words, rotating POV across the three main characters (~10 each), with 2–3 optional exception chapters from Henry Crow, Lonzo Reed, or Karl Reinmar.

# The Breakwater Season — Chapter Map

## Vital Statistics

| Item | Value |
|------|-------|
| Total target words | ~80,000 |
| Chapters | 30 |
| Average words per chapter | ~2,650 |
| 4th-POV chapters (short) | 3 (~1,600–1,800 words each) |
| Standard chapters | 27 (~2,500–2,900 words each) |
| Timeline | October 1943 – Spring 1945 |

## POV Distribution

| Character | Chapters | Count |
|-----------|----------|-------|
| Eira Bowman | 1, 4, 7, 11, 14, 17, 21, 25, 28, 30 | 10 |
| Hannah Pfeiffer | 2, 5, 8, 12, 15, 18, 22, 26, 29 | 9 |
| Dr. Marcus Klein | 3, 6, 9, 13, 16, 20, 24, 27 | 8 |
| Lonzo Reed (exceptional) | 10 | 1 |
| Karl Reinmar (exceptional) | 19 | 1 |
| Henry Crow (exceptional) | 23 | 1 |

The rotation is asymmetric by design. Hannah's chapters are slightly fewer because her arc completes in Ch 29; Eira holds the final chapter as coda. Marcus gets 8 regular-length chapters, not 10, but his arc has no wasted motion.

---

## Arc Shape

### SETUP — Chapters 1–9
*The world is made. The body arrives. Three people begin carrying it.*

The body on the Coldwater breakwater is the inciting object. Within the first nine chapters all three POV worlds are established (harbor, farm, school), the body arrives, the POWs arrive on the Pfeiffer farm, and each character's central predicament is defined. The body has not yet become each person's private problem; it is still a public event. The setup ends when that changes.

### RISING ACTION — Chapters 10–18
*Each character moves toward the body's meaning by a private route. Weight accumulates.*

Through a Maine winter and into spring 1944, the three carry what they know and don't say separately. Relationships deepen: Hannah and Karl through the fence; Marcus and Eira through a careful professional encounter; Marcus and Henry through chess and the long rhythm of the Crow household. Information trickles in — a dory, a stranger in Portland, a letter from Cambridge — without resolving. The body's probable identity as a European refugee trying to reach America begins to form in shadow.

### TURN — Chapters 19–24
*D-Day (June 6, 1944) and its aftermath. Each arc reaches its crisis point.*

D-Day breaks the long suspension of the war-as-pressure and makes the ending imaginable. For each character this triggers the question they have been avoiding: What does Eira do with what she knows? What does Karl's past actually mean to Hannah? What does Coldwater mean to Marcus — and does he stay? The three exceptional 4th-POV chapters (Lonzo, Karl, Henry) are placed in this block because outsider perspectives are most clarifying at the crisis point.

### CLIMAX — Chapters 25–27
*The decisive choices are made. One year after the body.*

In October 1944 — one year after the body — Eira makes her choice: deliberate silence, not omission. Hannah says goodbye to Karl at the end of the farm season with the specific clarity of something that was exactly what it was. Marcus declines a return to Cambridge, chooses Coldwater, and stops waiting.

### CODA — Chapters 28–30
*Spring 1945. Resolutions smaller and older than the war's ending.*

The war ends in the background. Will comes home changed. The official case closes on paper. A woman with a photograph comes to Eira's office looking for someone; Eira tells her the truth as she has arranged it. The harbor opens for spring. The story ends where Eira is standing, at the water, the log open, the pen down.

---

## Chapter Index

| # | POV | Date (approx.) | Title | One-Line Summary | Arc Phase |
|---|-----|----------------|-------|-----------------|-----------|
| 01 | Eira | Oct 11–14, 1943 | The Night Watch | Eira sees an unidentified man on the north wharf at 2 a.m.; three days later his body comes in on the breakwater. | Setup |
| 02 | Hannah | mid-Oct 1943 | Arrival | Four German POWs arrive at Pfeiffer farm; Hannah sees Karl last off the truck. | Setup |
| 03 | Marcus | late Oct 1943 | The Question Nobody Asked | Marcus hears about the body through school gossip and privately wonders what he won't say aloud. | Setup |
| 04 | Eira | Nov 1943 | What the Ledger Holds | The Coast Guard returns with news the dead man was not American; Eira answers their questions and says nothing about October 11. | Setup |
| 05 | Hannah | Nov 1943 | The Bavarian Accent | Hannah speaks to Karl through the fence for the first time; Albrecht hears the dialect from the kitchen window. | Setup |
| 06 | Marcus | Nov 1943 | English Only | The school board prohibits German in the German classroom; Marcus complies in the letter and starts to understand Hannah's situation. | Setup |
| 07 | Eira | Dec 1943 | December Protocol | A Naval Intelligence man from Portland comes asking questions; Eira gives him nothing new and catalogs what she remembers. | Setup |
| 08 | Hannah | Dec 1943–Jan 1944 | Shorter Letters | Will's V-Mails stop arriving; Hannah runs the farm through the silence and her conversations with Karl deepen. | Setup |
| 09 | Marcus | Jan 1944 | What a Spy Would Say | A student asks Marcus outright if he's a spy; he answers with precision; afterward he tells Henry and Henry's response is the chapter's turn. | Setup |
| 10 | Lonzo | Feb 1944 | Lonzo's War | Lonzo observes Hannah and Karl's fence conversations, considers reporting it, decides this isn't his war anymore. | Rising |
| 11 | Eira | Feb 1944 | Portland | Eira drives to Portland on a pretext and asks a customs clerk about refugees crossing from Canada; she learns nothing and understands more. | Rising |
| 12 | Hannah | Feb 1944 | Bread | Magda falls; Hannah manages the farm alone for a day; Karl tells her the thing he misses most is bread that was right. | Rising |
| 13 | Marcus | Mar 1944 | What Meaning Costs | Hannah submits a remarkable essay on translation; Marcus meets with her after class; a letter arrives about European nationals who passed through the Maritime Provinces. | Rising |
| 14 | Eira | Mar–Apr 1944 | The Dory | A lobsterman mentions offhand that he saw a foreign dory pulled up on the north breakwater beach in early October; Eira goes to look. | Rising |
| 15 | Hannah | Apr 1944 | Will's Letter | The first letter from Will in months arrives; Albrecht cries silently at the table; Hannah goes out to the field; Karl stands at the fence at dusk. | Rising |
| 16 | Marcus | Apr–May 1944 | Licensing | Marcus and Eira meet for the first time over a rowboat storage permit; neither names what both are thinking; both leave knowing more. | Rising |
| 17 | Eira | May 1944 | The Log Entry | A French-Canadian sailor's manner of speaking triggers something; Eira goes home and reads what she wrote in the log for October 11. | Rising |
| 18 | Hannah | May–Jun 1944 | The Columbine | Hannah turns eighteen; Karl places a wildflower on the fence post; she takes it and is angry with herself for taking it. | Rising |
| 19 | Karl | Jun 6–7, 1944 | Which End | D-Day through Karl's single day on the farm; he hears it on the radio; in the icehouse a fellow prisoner asks what it means for them. | Turn |
| 20 | Marcus | Jun 1944 | Paris, 1940 | D-Day empties the school; Marcus sits alone in his classroom approaching the memory of Paris without entering it; Henry comes to find him. | Turn |
| 21 | Eira | Jul 1944 | The Stranger at the Dock | A civilian — European-cut coat — asks at the harbor about the October body; says he's looking for a relative; Eira sends him to Portland; the picture assembles further. | Turn |
| 22 | Hannah | Jul–Aug 1944 | Southern France | Karl, unprompted, tells Hannah something about his posting before capture; she understands what he means; she does not come back to the fence that week. | Turn |
| 23 | Henry | Aug–Sep 1944 | The Photograph | Henry, fixing Marcus's window frame, finds a photograph of men in a courtyard with barbed wire, replaces it exactly, and that evening lets Marcus win at chess. | Turn |
| 24 | Marcus | Sep–Oct 1944 | Contract Review | The school board calls for an early contract review; Marcus prepares his case; a letter offers a return to Cambridge; he puts it in a drawer. | Turn |
| 25 | Eira | Oct 1944 | One Year | One year after the body; Eira goes out to the breakwater alone; she assembles what she knows and makes the decision that her silence will be permanent and chosen. | Climax |
| 26 | Hannah | Oct–Nov 1944 | Last Day | The POW detail ends for the season; Karl's last day on the farm; Hannah goes to the fence at the end of the day and says, in German, what she can say. | Climax |
| 27 | Marcus | Nov–Dec 1944 | Cornbread | Marcus writes the letter declining Cambridge, survives the contract review, tells Henry and Lillian he is staying; he eats Lillian's cornbread and cannot find words. | Climax |
| 28 | Eira | Jan–Feb 1945 | Filed | A letter from Portland formally closes the October 1943 case; Eira files it, opens the log, makes the day's entry; the case is closed; the harbor is not. | Coda |
| 29 | Hannah | Mar–Apr 1945 | The Good Cloth | Will comes home alive and changed; Albrecht speaks English at the dinner table for the first time in years; Magda sets the good cloth; Hannah thinks of Karl without grief. | Coda |
| 30 | Eira | Apr–May 1945 | All Moorings Secure | Spring harbor opening; a woman with a photograph asks Eira if she has seen the man in it; Eira says no; the harbor opens; the log is written; the war ends in the background. | Coda |
